context: The survey frames automatic text summarization as a response to information overload, arguing that good summaries can reduce reading time and improve retrieval precision by focusing indexing and attention on a document's main ideas. It organizes the literature around query-relevant versus generic summarization and around sentence extraction versus abstraction, then concentrates on extractive methods. After reviewing early heuristic approaches based on frequency, cue words, titles, and sentence position, it compares later unsupervised generic methods such as cosine similarity, relevance measure, latent semantic analysis with SVD, maximal marginal relevance, full-coverage selection, MEAD, and clustering-based methods. The survey highlights their strengths and weaknesses: some favor relevance but miss topic diversity, others improve coverage or redundancy control at the cost of precision or computational efficiency. It also reviews evaluation methods including precision and recall, kappa, relative utility, and BLEU or n-gram style measures. The overall conclusion is that existing methods remain incomplete and that robust, broadly effective summarization for mixed document collections still requires better approaches.

candidate_answer:
# A Survey of Unstructured Text Summarization Techniques

## Preliminary synthesis

The survey presents automatic text summarization as a practical response to information overload and to the low precision of searching full documents instead of focused summaries. It distinguishes query-oriented summaries from generic summaries, and extractive approaches from more difficult sentence abstraction methods, while concentrating mainly on extractive and especially unsupervised generic summarization.

The review traces early feature-based methods built on frequency, cue phrases, title words, and sentence position, then compares later approaches such as cosine similarity, relevance-measure ranking, latent semantic analysis, maximal marginal relevance, full-coverage selection, MEAD, and clustering-based summarization. Across these methods, the paper stresses the trade-off between relevance, diversity, redundancy control, and computational cost.

It also summarizes common evaluation methods, including precision and recall, kappa, relative utility, and BLEU or n-gram based metrics. Its overall conclusion is that no single summarization approach works well across all document types, so stronger and more general methods are still needed.
